items.q: What are the 4 Cs of food hygiene?
items.correct: Cleaning, Cooking, Chilling, and avoiding Cross-contamination.
items.why: Students guess at the Cs. They specifically address the four food-safety risks: dirty surfaces, undercooked food, warm storage, and pathogens transferring between foods (raw to ready-to-eat).

items.q: On a traffic-light label, what does a red light indicate?
items.correct: High amounts of that nutrient per 100g - eat less often.
items.misconception: The food is dangerous and should never be eaten.
items.why: Students treat red as 'forbidden'. Traffic lights are a guidance system - red = high, eat less often or in small portions. Many foods (cheese, olive oil) are red on certain nutrients but fine in moderation.

items.q: What does freezing do to bacteria in food?
items.correct: Makes them dormant (slows or halts growth), but does not kill them.
items.misconception: Kills all bacteria in the food.
items.why: Students think freezing sterilises food. It just stops bacteria multiplying - they revive when thawed. That's why thawed food should be cooked promptly and not refrozen raw.

items.q: If bread has a small patch of mould, is it safe to cut it off and eat the rest?
items.correct: No - mould roots and toxins can spread invisibly through soft foods like bread, so the loaf should be discarded.
items.misconception: Yes - mould only exists where you can see it, so the rest is fine.
items.random_wrong: Mouldy bread is safe if toasted.
items.why: The visible fuzz is only the mould's fruiting body; thread-like hyphae and possible mycotoxins penetrate soft, moist foods well beyond the visible patch. FSA advice: do not eat bread that is visibly mouldy. (Some hard cheeses are an exception - mould can be cut away with a margin.)

items.q: If you get food poisoning, was it definitely caused by the last meal you ate?
items.correct: Not necessarily - onset times range from about 30 minutes to several days depending on the bacteria, so an earlier meal may be the cause.
items.misconception: Yes - food poisoning always strikes within a couple of hours of the meal responsible.
items.random_wrong: Food poisoning can only come from restaurant food.
items.why: Students assume immediate cause and effect. Staphylococcus aureus toxins can act in 1-6 hours, but Salmonella often takes 12-72 hours and Campylobacter 2-5 days - so the culprit meal may have been days earlier.

items.q: How many portions of fruit and veg a day are recommended?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 5. “10” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Eatwell Guide is from which country?
items.why: Correct answer: UK. “USA” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Recommended portions of oily fish per week?
items.why: The real figure is 1 portion. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
